# Safety, Bias, and Ethical Considerations

## Hallucination and Factual Accuracy

Hallucination — the generation of plausible but factually incorrect content — represents the most clinically consequential limitation of LLMs in healthcare. The magnitude of this risk varies substantially by task complexity. In a benchmarking study of agentic systems, Liu et al. found that 89.9% of hallucinations were filtered by in-agent safeguards, yet a residual fraction persisted even under architectures specifically designed to suppress them [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. LLMs evaluating nomogram-based sentinel lymph node risk in breast cancer produced exact agreement with physician calculations in only 0–10% of cases without prompt engineering [@Shah2026Integrating], and zero-shot accuracy on ICD-9 medical coding collapsed to 14.6%–42.6% across models — far below the 79%–85% achieved on primary diagnosis — underscoring that numeric and structured-output tasks carry substantially higher hallucination risk than free-text generation [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating].

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as the most reproducible mitigation: a GraphRAG-enabled local LLM for gestational diabetes mellitus achieved BERTScore of 0.98, explicitly attributing freedom from hallucinations to grounding in a knowledge graph built from 1,212 peer-reviewed articles [@Evangelista2026GraphRAGEnabled], while a RAG system for nuclear medicine similarly improved factual precision over unaugmented generation [@Fukui2025Evaluation]. Human review remains indispensable regardless of architecture: a ChatGPT-4 pilot in the pediatric emergency department found no hallucinations in final transcripts only because physicians independently verified every output before clinical use [@MorleyFletcher2026A]. A distinct threat extends beyond text: Tordjman et al. demonstrated that radiologists could not reliably distinguish ChatGPT-generated synthetic radiographs from authentic images, raising concerns about AI-driven diagnostic fraud and the need for provenance verification in medical imaging archives [@Tordjman2026The].

## Bias and Equity

Demographic bias constitutes a structural risk that may silently widen existing health disparities. The systematic review of 39 medical LLM benchmarks by Gong et al. found that only 18 incorporated any non-English content, and performance on non-Western clinical scenarios remains poorly characterized despite benchmarks nominally spanning six continents [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. A qualitative study in child and adolescent psychiatry found that some models defaulted to generic Western ethical schemas when confronted with Turkish statutory obligations and sociocultural norms, an embodiment of training data bias that could produce clinically inappropriate guidance in non-Western settings [@en2025Artificial]. A distinct form of bias — "safety bias" — was documented by Polus et al. in free-tier LLMs responding to ESC hypertension guideline questions: all three models defaulted to over-medicalization in complex scenarios despite achieving 80%–82.5% overall accuracy, a directional error that may be as consequential as factual failures in patients vulnerable to polypharmacy [@Polus2026Adherence]. Addressing these patterns requires curation of more representative training corpora, domain-specific fine-tuning on diverse clinical populations, and prospective subgroup evaluation as a precondition for deployment authorization rather than a post-hoc audit.

## Reporting and Transparency

The absence of standardized reporting for LLM-based studies has generated a literature characterized by methodological heterogeneity that impedes synthesis. Luo et al. applied six LLMs to assess adherence of 41 AI-interventional RCTs to the CONSORT-AI checklist and found that Item 2 — specifying input data inclusion and exclusion criteria — received the lowest compliance across all models (mean Overall Consistency Score 48.8%), exposing a systematic blind spot in how AI clinical trials document their data pipelines [@Luo2025Using]. The REFINE guideline — Reporting checklist for Foundation and Large Language Models in Medical Research — was subsequently published as an international consensus covering model provenance, fine-tuning procedures, prompt reproducibility, and evaluation methodology [@Mese2026Reporting]. Reproducibility challenges are compounded by the opacity of proprietary model weights, making independent replication of most published studies effectively impossible. The MedHELM framework addresses this gap by benchmarking nine frontier LLMs across 121 clinically specific tasks with an automated jury evaluation method; advanced reasoning models achieved win rates of 66%, while a more economical model reached comparable performance at 15% lower computational cost — a finding with direct relevance to evidence-based model selection in resource-constrained health systems [@Bedi2026Holistic].

## Regulatory and Governance Frameworks

The regulatory landscape for clinical LLMs remains nascent and fragmented. In the United States, the FDA has classified many AI/ML-based clinical decision support tools as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD), requiring premarket review for higher-risk applications; however, general-purpose LLMs deployed for documentation or education frequently fall outside current regulatory pathways. Afshar et al. demonstrated the value of embedding governance structures prospectively: a bidirectional governance model linking operations and research through multidisciplinary workgroups reduced ICD-10 documentation accuracy degradation following a note template change — initially falling from 79% (95% CI 72%–86%) to 35% (95% CI 28%–42%) before recovering to baseline after targeted redesign — by enabling real-time monitoring and iterative correction [@Afshar2025A]. This pragmatic trial framework, anchored in the Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety, represents a model for responsible ambient AI deployment in health systems.

Professional societies have begun issuing specialty-specific guidance. The European Federation of Internal Medicine's Delphi panel, reaching unanimous consensus among thirty international experts, identified clinical validation, algorithmic transparency, and alignment with P6 Medicine principles as preconditions for responsible deployment [@SaidCriado2025Advancing]. Specialists in radiology [@Park2025Clinical] and otolaryngology [@Bao2026Large] have similarly called for domain-specific fine-tuning, secure multi-institutional deployment, and legal oversight as prerequisites for clinical translation. The TrialMatchAI clinical trial matching system illustrates proactive governance through fine-tuned open-source models in a RAG framework that delivers traceable decision rationales, local deployment compatibility, and over 90% accuracy in eligibility classification while preserving data privacy [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI]. Federated learning frameworks that train models across distributed hospital datasets without centralizing patient-identifiable data offer a complementary governance mechanism for privacy-preserving clinical AI [@Lal2026Ollamadriven].

## Education and Training

Medical education represents both a promising application domain and a source of cautionary evidence for LLM deployment. Gustafsson et al. conducted a randomized controlled pilot trial in which 41 final-year medical students were allocated to LLM-assisted or conventional trauma simulation sessions. Contrary to expectations, LLM access did not enhance decision accuracy or speed and was associated with longer response times in complex scenarios; teams without LLM access demonstrated more active peer discussion and superior teamwork scores (median 5.0 vs. 3.5; p = 0.08), suggesting that cognitive offloading to AI may impair the collaborative reasoning skills essential to clinical practice [@Gustafsson2026Integrating]. Students primarily used the LLM for fact-checking but frequently encountered vague or overly general responses — a pattern consistent with the knowledge-practice gap documented across 39 benchmarks, where LLMs routinely exceed average physician performance on USMLE-style examinations (84%–90% accuracy) yet achieve only 45%–69% on practice-based assessments and fall to 40%–50% on safety-critical clinical tasks [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice].

These findings expose a central educational challenge: LLM evaluation is saturated by knowledge-based metrics that do not predict clinical competence. Lin and Cho found that source-based LLMs using student-uploaded RAG content did not consistently outperform baseline models on USMLE Step 1 dermatology questions, and model choice influenced accuracy and reproducibility in ways not easily predicted from general benchmarks [@Lin2026Evaluating]. For patient-facing education, LLMs demonstrated moderate-to-high reliability on perinatal depression FAQs (ICC 0.729–0.847), yet readability consistently exceeded public health literacy benchmarks, raising equity concerns about reach among patients with lower literacy [@Huang2026Can]. Training healthcare professionals in AI literacy — encompassing prompt engineering, critical appraisal of model outputs, recognition of hallucination patterns, and understanding of bias mechanisms — has consequently emerged as an urgent workforce priority, as clinicians remain the indispensable human-in-the-loop safeguard that all current evidence identifies as a prerequisite for safe clinical LLM deployment.
